title: 5 Movies That Flopped In Theaters — Then Became Cult Classics
dek: One of these is regularly voted the greatest movie ever made. It barely broke even.

items.heading: The Shawshank Redemption barely made its money back in theaters

images.caption: A still from The Shawshank Redemption

items.text: Now a fixture at the top of best-movies-ever-made lists, Shawshank was a genuine box office disappointment on release in 1994 — its dour title and prison setting were widely blamed for keeping audiences away. It found its real audience later, through home video and cable reruns.

items.heading: Blade Runner barely broke even against its budget
items.text: Ridley Scott's now-foundational sci-fi noir made only around $27.6 million domestically against a roughly $28 million budget in 1982 — a modest showing that's a big reason a sequel didn't arrive for 35 years. Its reputation has only grown since, largely thanks to later director's cuts.

items.heading: The Big Lebowski made just $18 million domestically on a $15 million budget

images.caption: A still from The Big Lebowski

items.text: The Coen brothers' shaggy-dog noir comedy underwhelmed on release in 1998, overshadowed by the acclaim for their previous film, Fargo. It's since become one of the most quoted and re-watched comedies of its era, complete with fan festivals devoted entirely to it.

items.heading: Fight Club was a disappointment in the U.S. before finding its audience on DVD
items.text: David Fincher's Fight Club earned only around $37 million domestically against a $63 million budget in 1999, a genuine underperformance in its home market. International grosses helped it turn a modest profit overall, but its real cultural life began once it hit home video.

items.heading: It's a Wonderful Life actually lost RKO money in 1946

images.caption: A still from It's a Wonderful Life

items.text: Frank Capra's now-beloved Christmas classic needed to double its budget just to break even, and it fell short, recording a loss of roughly $525,000 for RKO on its original release. It became a holiday staple decades later largely because its copyright briefly lapsed, letting TV stations air it for free every December.
